rationale_for_baseline_tier: Critical, refractory hypoxaemia (home SpO2 84% not correcting) with central cyanosis, new drowsiness and respiratory-muscle exhaustion after an aspiration event is an immediate threat to life with impending respiratory arrest. The patient needs high-flow oxygen and advanced airway/ventilatory support that only an emergency ambulance and hospital can provide — T5 (call 000, emergency ambulance) is the only appropriate tier. The source clinician called 000 for an Intensive Care Paramedic and anticipated pre-hospital intubation; the telehealth-correct action mirrors that escalation.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Refractory hypoxaemia (SpO2 in the 80s not improving) with central cyanosis, drowsiness and severe respiratory distress
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — call 000 immediately, do not wait or trial home measures
escalation_edges.rationale: This constellation is critical hypoxaemic respiratory failure; every minute of delay increases the risk of respiratory arrest and death.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This is a breathing emergency. Call 000 now for an ambulance and tell them he is severely short of breath with an oxygen level of 84 percent and blue lips. Keep him sitting upright and calm, and stay with him.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Increasing exhaustion, becoming unresponsive, or stopping breathing normally
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — stay on the line with 000
escalation_edges.rationale: A tiring patient in high-effort respiratory failure can arrest suddenly; bystander CPR with dispatcher guidance is the critical intervention.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he becomes unresponsive or stops breathing normally, tell 000 right away, lay him flat on the floor and start CPR — the call-taker will talk you through the chest compressions.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any temptation to manage at home (diuretic, inhaler, continue antibiotics, wait for a daytime clinic)
escalation_edges.rationale: Home/next-day pathways are unsafe here; the required treatments cannot be delivered remotely and delay is lethal. This edge exists to block under-triage.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Please do not wait for a GP appointment or try tablets at home — this needs the hospital now. Call 000.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Bedbound patient with a single live-in carer who cannot manage the respiratory distress alone
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give the carer clear, simple, sequential instructions: call 000, keep him upright and calm, unlock the door for paramedics, watch his breathing and consciousness, and be ready for CPR guidance. Do not assume the carer can lift, ventilate or transport him.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Older, frail, bedbound adult with aspiration risk
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower physiological reserve — deterioration can be rapid; escalate early and do not be reassured by any brief plateau.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an emergency ambulance right now and say he is in severe breathing difficulty with an oxygen level of 84 percent and blue lips. Keep him sitting upright, calm and as still as possible, and minimise talking to save his oxygen. Stay with him, unlock the front door for the paramedics, and keep watching his breathing and how awake he is.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours or a daytime appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary detailing the lung injury, its aspiration/infective driver and the ventilatory support given; take this and the aspiration-risk plan to the GP for follow-up.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating refractory hypoxaemia with cyanosis and drowsiness as something manageable at home or via a GP appointment — e.g. anchoring on heart failure and advising a fluid tablet, or on 'chest infection' and continuing home antibiotics.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Delay to oxygen and ventilatory support results in respiratory arrest and death that emergency transfer would have prevented.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising the patient go to an emergency department by their own transport rather than calling an ambulance — a critically hypoxic, cyanotic patient could arrest during unsupported self-transport with no oxygen or resuscitation capability available.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: For this case T5 is correct, not over-triage; there is effectively no clinically meaningful over-triage risk — the cost of over-escalating a critically hypoxic patient is negligible against the cost of under-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The exact aetiology (non-cardiogenic vs cardiogenic oedema vs aspiration pneumonia/sepsis) cannot be resolved by telehealth — the discriminating findings (JVP, S3, BNP, chest X-ray) are all clinician/investigation-derived.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate on the unambiguous life-threat (refractory hypoxaemia) without committing to an aetiology, and explicitly avoid the aetiology-specific home treatments (diuretic/inhaler) that could be harmful or cause delay.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Resolving the uncertainty prematurely into 'heart failure' or 'chest infection' and giving aetiology-specific home advice instead of calling 000.
